expanded_description: Congruent figures have exactly the same size and shape. For triangles, this means all three pairs of corresponding sides and all three pairs of corresponding angles are congruent. The acronym CPCTC—Corresponding Parts of Congruent Triangles are Congruent—is the logical consequence of triangle congruence: once two triangles are proven congruent, every remaining pair of corresponding parts is automatically congruent. CPCTC is the payoff step in countless geometric proofs.

core_concept.principle: Two triangles are congruent if there is a correspondence between their vertices such that all six pairs of corresponding parts (three sides and three angles) match. Proving triangle congruence requires only three of these six pairs—using SSS, SAS, ASA, AAS, or HL—and CPCTC then guarantees the other three. CPCTC is not a theorem to be proved; it is the direct logical consequence of the definition of congruent triangles.
core_concept.explanation: Congruent triangles are identical in every measurable way. When you prove two triangles congruent, you have shown they are the same triangle in two different positions. CPCTC lets you claim that any specific pair of corresponding parts—a side you need to prove equal, or an angle you need to prove congruent—matches. The flow is always: prove the triangles congruent first, then apply CPCTC to get the specific conclusion you need. Never use CPCTC before triangle congruence is established.

image.alt: Two congruent triangles with vertices labeled and corresponding parts color-coded. A flowchart: Prove triangles congruent (SSS/SAS/ASA/AAS/HL) → CPCTC → desired conclusion.
image.caption: CPCTC: Once triangles are proven congruent, all corresponding parts are guaranteed congruent.

sections.introduction: Congruence means identical in size and shape. For triangles, this requires all corresponding sides and angles to match. The order of vertices in a congruence statement defines which parts correspond—writing $\triangle ABC \cong \triangle DEF$ means $A$ matches $D$, $B$ matches $E$, $C$ matches $F$. Getting the correspondence right is essential for correctly identifying which parts CPCTC can prove congruent.

introduction_image.alt: Two congruent triangles with vertices labeled. Arrows connecting A↔D, B↔E, C↔F. All six pairs of corresponding parts marked with matching symbols.
introduction_image.caption: Congruent triangles: corresponding vertices listed in matching order. All six pairs of parts match.

key_concept.definition: Two triangles are congruent if and only if all three pairs of corresponding sides and all three pairs of corresponding angles are congruent. The statement $\triangle ABC \cong \triangle DEF$ establishes the correspondence $A \leftrightarrow D$, $B \leftrightarrow E$, $C \leftrightarrow F$, and consequently $\overline{AB} \cong \overline{DE}$, $\overline{BC} \cong \overline{EF}$, $\overline{CA} \cong \overline{FD}$, $\angle A \cong \angle D$, $\angle B \cong \angle E$, $\angle C \cong \angle F$. The vertex order in the congruence statement is essential—it encodes the correspondence.
key_concept.context: Once triangle congruence is proved using a criterion (SSS, SAS, ASA, AAS, HL), CPCTC allows concluding any remaining corresponding parts are congruent. This is the standard path to proving a specific segment or angle relationship in geometric proofs.

image.alt: Two triangles with congruence statement annotated. A table listing all six pairs of corresponding parts. Arrows showing which parts follow from the congruence criterion and which follow from CPCTC.
image.caption: Congruence statement defines correspondence. Order of vertices is essential.

process_flow.description: Determine which vertex of the first triangle pairs with which vertex of the second. This comes from the given congruent parts or from the order in a congruence statement.
process_flow.mechanism: If given $\overline{AB} \cong \overline{XY}$, then $A \leftrightarrow X$ and $B \leftrightarrow Y$. If given $\angle A \cong \angle D$, then $A \leftrightarrow D$. Build the complete correspondence from the given pairs. In $\triangle ABC \cong \triangle PQR$, the correspondence is $A \leftrightarrow P$, $B \leftrightarrow Q$, $C \leftrightarrow R$.
process_flow.result: A complete vertex correspondence between the two triangles.
applications: Given $\overline{AB} \cong \overline{PQ}$ and $\overline{BC} \cong \overline{QR}$, the correspondence is $A \leftrightarrow P$, $B \leftrightarrow Q$, $C \leftrightarrow R$.

image.alt: Two triangles with given congruent parts marked. Arrows connecting corresponding vertices. Congruence statement with order highlighted.
image.caption: Step 1: Establish the vertex correspondence from given congruent parts.

process_flow.description: Use SSS, SAS, ASA, AAS, or HL to establish triangle congruence. This requires identifying three pairs of corresponding parts that satisfy one of the criteria.
process_flow.mechanism: Assemble the three required pairs—from given information, the Reflexive Property (shared sides), the Vertical Angles Theorem, alternate interior angles (parallel lines), the Definition of Midpoint, or the Definition of Angle Bisector. State the triangle congruence with the correct criterion cited.
process_flow.result: A proven statement that the two triangles are congruent.

image.alt: Two triangles with three pairs of parts highlighted that satisfy a congruence criterion. The criterion named and the congruence statement written.
image.caption: Step 2: Prove the triangles congruent using an appropriate criterion.

process_flow.description: Once triangle congruence is proved, CPCTC justifies concluding that any other corresponding parts are congruent. This is where the proof reaches its target conclusion.
process_flow.mechanism: After stating the triangles are congruent, write the specific conclusion needed—a segment congruence or angle congruence—and cite CPCTC as the reason. This step proves the geometric fact the problem originally asked for.
process_flow.result: The specific segment or angle congruence that was the goal of the proof.
applications: '$M$ is the midpoint — CPCTC and Definition of Midpoint.'

image.alt: From the congruence statement, an arrow labeled CPCTC points to the specific conclusion. The conclusion matches the Prove statement.
image.caption: Step 3: Use CPCTC to conclude specific corresponding parts are congruent.

classifications.value: CPCTC is used to prove specific geometric facts after triangle congruence is established.
characteristics: Proving segments congruent (often to show a triangle is isosceles).
Proving angles congruent (often to show lines are parallel via alternate interior angles).
Proving a point is a midpoint (by showing two collinear segments are congruent).
Proving an angle is bisected (by showing two adjacent angles are congruent).
CPCTC is always the step AFTER triangle congruence, never before.
classifications.behavior: CPCTC is the payoff of a congruence proof. The work is in establishing triangle congruence; CPCTC delivers the specific conclusion needed. It cannot be used before triangle congruence is proved.

examples.process: Proving a point is a midpoint using CPCTC
examples.explanation: Given intersecting segments forming two triangles. Prove the triangles congruent by SAS. By CPCTC, two corresponding segments are congruent. Since the point lies between them on the same line, the point is the midpoint by definition.

image.alt: Two overlapping triangles sharing a vertex. SAS proof leads to CPCTC, which proves two segments equal, establishing a midpoint.
image.caption: CPCTC used to prove a point is a midpoint: congruent segments → midpoint.

image.alt: Complete section overview: definition of congruent triangles, vertex correspondence, three-step process, and common CPCTC applications.
image.caption: Congruent Figures and CPCTC overview: correspondence, proof, and the CPCTC conclusion.

sections.introduction: The two-column proof format clearly separates the triangle congruence step from the CPCTC step. The logical flow is: given parts → derived parts → triangle congruence → CPCTC → conclusion. This structure appears in dozens of geometry theorems.

introduction_image.alt: Two-column proof template showing the standard sequence. Highlighted rows for the triangle congruence statement and the CPCTC conclusion.
introduction_image.caption: CPCTC in two-column proofs: the final step after triangle congruence is established.

key_concept.definition: In a two-column proof, CPCTC appears as the reason for a statement that concludes specific corresponding parts are congruent. It can only be used after a statement asserting the two triangles are congruent. The typical order is: state given congruences, prove additional congruences (reflexive, vertical angles), state the triangles are congruent citing the criterion, conclude the desired parts congruent citing CPCTC.
key_concept.context: This pattern is universal for triangle-based proofs. Mastering the CPCTC two-column structure is essential for success in Unit 4 and beyond.

image.alt: Complete example proof with diagram. All statements and reasons shown, with the CPCTC step highlighted as the final conclusion.
image.caption: Standard CPCTC proof structure: givens → additional congruences → triangle congruence → CPCTC.

worked_examples.title: CPCTC Proof Practice
worked_examples.problem:
Write complete two-column proofs.

1) Given: $\overline{AB} \cong \overline{CB}$, $\overline{AD} \cong \overline{CD}$. Prove: $\angle A \cong \angle C$.
2) Given: $M$ is the midpoint of $\overline{AB}$ and $\overline{CD}$. Prove: $\overline{AC} \cong \overline{BD}$.
3) Given: $\overline{AB} \cong \overline{AC}$, $\overline{AD}$ bisects $\angle BAC$. Prove: $\overline{BD} \cong \overline{CD}$.

step_by_step.explanation:
Statements | Reasons
1) $\overline{AB} \cong \overline{CB}$ | Given
2) $\overline{AD} \cong \overline{CD}$ | Given
3) $\overline{BD} \cong \overline{BD}$ | Reflexive Property
4) $\triangle ABD \cong \triangle CBD$ | SSS
5) $\angle A \cong \angle C$ | CPCTC

Statements | Reasons
1) $M$ is midpoint of $\overline{AB}$ | Given
2) $\overline{AM} \cong \overline{MB}$ | Definition of Midpoint
3) $M$ is midpoint of $\overline{CD}$ | Given
4) $\overline{CM} \cong \overline{MD}$ | Definition of Midpoint
5) $\angle AMC \cong \angle BMD$ | Vertical Angles Theorem
6) $\triangle AMC \cong \triangle BMD$ | SAS
7) $\overline{AC} \cong \overline{BD}$ | CPCTC

Statements | Reasons
1) $\overline{AB} \cong \overline{AC}$ | Given
2) $\overline{AD}$ bisects $\angle BAC$ | Given
3) $\angle BAD \cong \angle CAD$ | Definition of Angle Bisector
4) $\overline{AD} \cong \overline{AD}$ | Reflexive Property
5) $\triangle ABD \cong \triangle ACD$ | SAS
6) $\overline{BD} \cong \overline{CD}$ | CPCTC

image.alt: Three diagrams with complete two-column proofs: (1) SSS → CPCTC for angles. (2) Midpoints + vertical angles → SAS → CPCTC for segments. (3) Angle bisector → SAS → CPCTC for segments.
image.caption: CPCTC proofs: SSS, SAS, and SAS leading to specific conclusions via CPCTC.

worked_examples.final_answer: See step-by-step for complete two-column proofs with CPCTC as the final step.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Two-column proofs using triangle congruence criteria followed by CPCTC to prove specific corresponding parts congruent.

sections.introduction: Mastery of CPCTC requires practice identifying corresponding parts, writing congruence statements with correct vertex order, and constructing complete proofs. Work through these problems to build fluency.

worked_examples.title: CPCTC Practice
worked_examples.problem:
1) If $\triangle ABC \cong \triangle DEF$, name all six pairs of congruent corresponding parts.
2) Write the congruence statement for two triangles where $A \leftrightarrow P$, $B \leftrightarrow Q$, $C \leftrightarrow R$.
3) Given: $\overline{AB} \cong \overline{DE}$, $\angle B \cong \angle E$, $\overline{BC} \cong \overline{EF}$. Prove: $\angle A \cong \angle D$.
4) Given: $\overline{AC} \cong \overline{BD}$, $\overline{AB} \cong \overline{CD}$. Prove: $\angle A \cong \angle D$. (Hint: draw diagonal $\overline{BC}$.)

step_by_step.explanation:
Sides: $\overline{AB} \cong \overline{DE}$, $\overline{BC} \cong \overline{EF}$, $\overline{CA} \cong \overline{FD}$.
Angles: $\angle A \cong \angle D$, $\angle B \cong \angle E$, $\angle C \cong \angle F$.
$\triangle ABC \cong \triangle PQR$. Vertices must be listed in corresponding order.

Statements | Reasons
1) $\overline{AB} \cong \overline{DE}$ | Given
2) $\angle B \cong \angle E$ | Given
3) $\overline{BC} \cong \overline{EF}$ | Given
4) $\triangle ABC \cong \triangle DEF$ | SAS
5) $\angle A \cong \angle D$ | CPCTC

Draw diagonal $\overline{BC}$.
Statements | Reasons
1) $\overline{AC} \cong \overline{BD}$ | Given
2) $\overline{AB} \cong \overline{CD}$ | Given
3) $\overline{BC} \cong \overline{CB}$ | Reflexive Property
4) $\triangle ABC \cong \triangle DCB$ | SSS
5) $\angle A \cong \angle D$ | CPCTC

image.alt: Four panels: (1) Two congruent triangles with all six pairs labeled. (2) Congruence statement with corresponding order. (3) SAS proof with CPCTC. (4) SSS proof using a diagonal with CPCTC.
image.caption: CPCTC practice: identifying parts, writing statements, and constructing proofs.

worked_examples.final_answer:
1) Six pairs as listed above.
2) $\triangle ABC \cong \triangle PQR$.
3) $\angle A \cong \angle D$ by SAS → CPCTC.
4) $\angle A \cong \angle D$ by SSS (with diagonal) → CPCTC.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Vertex correspondence, congruence statements, and CPCTC as the concluding step in congruence proofs.

summary: Congruent triangles have all six pairs of corresponding parts matching. Key points: 1) A congruence statement defines vertex correspondence. $\triangle ABC \cong \triangle DEF$ means $A \leftrightarrow D$, $B \leftrightarrow E$, $C \leftrightarrow F$. 2) Proving congruence requires only three pairs of parts using SSS, SAS, ASA, AAS, or HL. 3) CPCTC—Corresponding Parts of Congruent Triangles are Congruent—is applied AFTER triangle congruence is established. It proves the remaining pairs are congruent. 4) Proof structure: givens → derived congruences → triangle congruence (cite criterion) → CPCTC → desired conclusion. 5) CPCTC is never used before triangle congruence is proved. It is the final logical step that delivers the target conclusion in most triangle-based proofs.

review_questions_image.alt: Concept map with 'Congruent Triangles and CPCTC' in center. Branches: Definition (6 pairs of corresponding parts), Correspondence (vertex order in congruence statement), Congruence Criteria (SSS, SAS, ASA, AAS, HL), CPCTC (conclusion after congruence, proves remaining parts), Proof Structure (givens → extra congruences → triangle ≅ → CPCTC).
review_questions_image.caption: Congruent Figures and Corresponding Parts Review: triangle congruence and the CPCTC proof structure.
